guardrail_evaluations.explanation: The effective discount of 33.3% exceeds the mid-market soft ceiling of 20%. This is a yellow flag that the AE should be prepared to justify with documented competitive context.
At 33.3% off list, this deal exceeds the 30% mid-market threshold that requires Finance sign-off before we can proceed. This deal cannot move to closed-won without that approval.

The ramp schedule spans 12 months — four times the 3-month mid-market ceiling — which obscures the effective discount and creates ASC 606 complexity. Finance sign-off is required before this structure can be approved.

Estimated gross margin on the proposed price is approximately 10%, far below the 40% warn threshold. The concession stack — 6 months free, waived $40K implementation, and a 33% list discount — has devastated effective margin.

At ~10% estimated gross margin, this deal falls well below the 25% absolute floor and is structurally non-viable as proposed. The deal must be reshaped before re-quoting — it cannot be approved at any level in its current form.

alternative_structures.label: A — Remove Free Period; Replace with Tiered Ramp + PS Line Item
alternative_structures.description: Eliminate the 6-month free period. Instead, structure a 3-month paid ramp (months 1–3 at 40% of full ACV, months 4–24 at full contracted rate). Re-scope implementation services as a separate $25K professional services SKU (not waived), allowing the core subscription discount to compress to 20%. The 90-day out clause is removed; a 12-month non-cancelable commitment replaces it.
alternative_structures.rationale: Reintroducing PS as a revenue line recovers $25K in full-margin services revenue, and eliminating the free period returns the deal to list-margin territory. The customer still gets a meaningful 20% discount and a structured ramp — the trade-off is losing the free period and the anytime out clause, which were the two highest-cost concessions.

alternative_structures.label: B — 36-Month Commit at 25% Discount; Expansion Pricing Lock Structured as MFN Cap
alternative_structures.description: Extend the term to 36 months (from 24), which justifies a 25% discount off list, bringing proposed price to $270K ACV. The 6-month free period is eliminated. Implementation services are waived as a goodwill gesture (preserving that win for Tessera's CRO), but the 90-day out clause is replaced with a 6-month termination notice requirement after month 18. The 2026 pricing lock for expansions is restructured as an MFN cap: expansion seats are locked at no more than 2026 list + 5% annually, only applicable if Tessera expands by ≥10 seats.
alternative_structures.rationale: A longer term de-risks churn exposure introduced by the 90-day out clause and improves TCV. The MFN cap is conditioned on growth, aligning Clay's upside with Tessera's success, and the expansion lock only kicks in at meaningful scale — reducing the revenue ceiling risk from an unconditional pricing freeze through 2027.

alternative_structures.label: C — Usage-Commit Hybrid with Overage Upside; Co-Marketing Credit in Lieu of Additional Discount
alternative_structures.description: Convert the deal to a hybrid model: a $220K annual usage-commit floor (39% discount) plus an overage rate of $X/enrichment unit beyond the commit. Eliminate the free period and out clause entirely. Replace the waived implementation services with a co-marketing credit worth $40K (case study, logo rights, reference calls) — this costs Clay near-zero but gives Tessera's new CRO a PR win on stack consolidation. Expansion pricing lock is retained but scoped only to committed seats, not net-new seats.
alternative_structures.rationale: The lower commit floor gives Tessera price certainty during their consolidation ramp, while the overage structure gives Clay meaningful upside as Tessera grows into the platform post-displacement. Co-marketing credit replaces waived services at effectively zero cost to Clay's P&L. The trade-off is a lower guaranteed floor ACV, but the overage mechanism means realized ACV likely exceeds the current $240K proposal if usage ramps as projected in a consolidation scenario.

pricing.reasoning_summary: This deal as structured is a hard block: effective discount of 33.3% triggers a Finance sign-off block, the 12-month ramp triggers a separate Finance sign-off block, and the estimated gross margin of approximately 10% breaches the 25% absolute floor — making the deal non-viable in its current form. The margin figure is computed under a working assumption that list price carries a 40% gross margin and COGS are fixed at 60% of list; the CFO should validate this baseline before relying on the 10% margin estimate as precise. Beyond the guardrail failures, the non-standard clause stack — 6-month free period, waived $40K implementation, 90-day anytime out, and a 2026 pricing lock through 2027 — compounds the structural risk well beyond what the headline discount alone communicates. Precedent deals in similar healthtech or enterprise new-logo contexts were closed at 11–16% discounts, underscoring how far outside the norm this proposal sits.

performance_obligations.name: Platform subscription (paid term)
performance_obligations.description: 24-month SaaS subscription to the sales engagement platform, with paid term commencing month 7 after a 6-month free period.
performance_obligations.rationale: Stand-ready obligation to provide SaaS access; distinct from implementation per ASC 606-10-25-19. Recognized ratably over the access period.

performance_obligations.name: Free-period platform access (months 1-6)
performance_obligations.description: Six months of complimentary platform access prior to the paid term start.
performance_obligations.rationale: Free period is not a separate PO but a material right/discount that must be allocated against total contract consideration; the combined 30-month service period drives ratable recognition of the $480K TCV.

performance_obligations.name: Implementation services (waived)
performance_obligations.description: Onboarding/implementation services normally priced at $40K, waived in this deal.
performance_obligations.rationale: Implementation is bundled and waived; if not separately identifiable from the SaaS (no standalone utility to customer), it is combined with the subscription PO under ASC 606-10-25-21.

performance_obligations.name: Expansion seat pricing lock (2026 rates through 2027)
performance_obligations.description: Customer option to purchase additional seats at 2026 pricing through end of 2027.
performance_obligations.rationale: Represents a material right under ASC 606-10-55-41 if pricing is incremental to standalone; a portion of transaction price may need to be allocated to this option and recognized when exercised or expired.

variable_consideration_flags.explanation: A 90-day anytime out clause may shorten the enforceable contract term under ASC 606-10-25-3, potentially limiting the contract to a rolling 90-day period and dramatically changing the revenue schedule.
The 2026-priced expansion lock through 2027 may constitute a material right requiring allocation of transaction price under ASC 606-10-55-42, estimated using expected exercise probability.
Free period is effectively a discount; total $480K TCV is allocated over the full 30-month service period (months 1-30) on a ratable basis, not just the paid 18 months.
Waived $40K implementation reduces standalone value of bundled services; if not distinct, it is subsumed into subscription PO with no separate allocation.

contract_modification_risk.explanation: The 90-day termination-for-convenience clause combined with the expansion pricing lock creates meaningful contract modification risk under ASC 606-10-25-10. If the customer exercises TFC, remaining performance is canceled and revenue must be reassessed; if they expand at locked pricing, the addition of distinct goods/services at non-standalone price triggers modification accounting (likely a prospective modification or termination-and-replacement).

recognized_revenue_schedule.notes: Total TCV of $480K allocated ratably over 30-month service period = $16K/month. Free period still requires ratable recognition because service is being delivered.
$16K/month ratable; cash collected $120K annual upfront but recognition continues straight-line.
$16K/month ratable across 12 months of paid term.
Final 6 months of the 30-month service period; balance of TCV recognized.

red_flags.label: 90-day termination-for-convenience clause
red_flags.explanation: TFC right may collapse the enforceable contract term to 90 days under ASC 606-10-25-3, materially altering revenue recognition, ARR reporting, and commission clawback exposure. Requires CFO and audit pre-clearance.

red_flags.label: Expansion pricing lock through 2027
red_flags.explanation: May create a material right requiring transaction price allocation; if 2026 pricing is below future SSP, deferred revenue treatment is required for the option.

red_flags.label: 6 months free + waived $40K implementation + 35% discount
red_flags.explanation: Stacked concessions complicate SSP determination and discount allocation across POs; the effective discount vs. list ($360K) is materially higher than headline 35% once free period and waived services are included.

red_flags.label: Recognition period exceeds stated 24-month term
red_flags.explanation: Service delivery spans 30 months (6 free + 24 paid), so ratable recognition crosses three fiscal years even though the paid term is 24 months.

asc606.reasoning_summary: This deal stacks multiple ASC 606 complexities: a 6-month free period that extends the service delivery window to 30 months, a waived implementation service likely combined with the subscription PO, a 90-day TFC clause that could shorten the enforceable term, and an expansion pricing lock that may constitute a material right requiring transaction price allocation. The interaction of these clauses makes the recognition schedule and transaction price allocation highly judgment-dependent. This is an automated estimate; final determination requires CFO/auditor review.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Six months of free access before the paid term begins; the 24-month paid term does not commence until month 7, meaning Clay receives zero revenue for the first half of year one.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: Clay bears full provisioning, support, and infrastructure cost for six months with no cash inflow. Combined with waived $40K implementation services, the real first-year yield on a $240K ACV deal drops to roughly $120K collected in months 7–12 — a 50% effective year-one haircut. If the customer churns at month 8 using the 90-day TFC clause, total cash collected is ~$40K against full cost-to-serve.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Free period shall be reduced to a maximum of sixty (60) days ('Onboarding Period'), commencing on the Effective Date. The Subscription Term and billing obligations shall commence on the first day of the third (3rd) calendar month following the Effective Date. No extension of the Onboarding Period shall be granted except by mutual written amendment signed by authorized representatives of both parties.
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept up to ninety (90) days free if (a) the 90-day TFC clause is removed entirely or converted to a for-cause-only right, and (b) annual upfront payment for months 4–12 is invoiced on Day 1 and due Net 30, not deferred to month 7.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has granted up to 60-day onboarding periods for competitive displacement deals; 6-month free periods have not been approved at this ACV tier.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Customer may terminate the agreement at any time for any reason with 90 days' written notice, with no termination fee or remaining obligation.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: A 90-day anytime-out in a 24-month deal functionally converts a $480K TCV contract into a rolling quarterly commitment. Stacked on top of the 6-month free period, the worst-case scenario is Tessera exits at month 9 (first 90-day notice window after paid term starts) having paid only ~$60K. This also sets a dangerous precedent for mid-market logo deals if Tessera references it in a future negotiation.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Neither party may terminate this Agreement for convenience during the Initial Term. Following the Initial Term, either party may terminate for convenience upon one hundred eighty (180) days' prior written notice. Any purported termination-for-convenience during the Initial Term shall be void and of no effect; Customer's sole remedy for Clay's material breach shall be the termination-for-cause procedure set forth in Section [X].
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: If TFC during term is a hard requirement, accept a 90-day TFC right that (a) is not exercisable until month 18 of the paid term (i.e., no earlier than month 24 inclusive of free period), (b) requires Customer to pay 50% of remaining committed fees as a termination fee, and (c) is waived entirely if Clay achieves mutually agreed onboarding milestones within 90 days of paid term start.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay's standard MSA does not include a TFC right during the Initial Term; have occasionally accepted post-year-1 TFC with a 3-month fee buyout at enterprise tier ($500K+ ACV) only.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Any seat or usage expansions purchased by Customer through December 31, 2027 shall be invoiced at the per-unit pricing in effect as of the 2026 contract Effective Date (i.e., the discounted rates under this Order Form).
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: This locks in a 35%-discounted rate for incremental seats for up to ~20 months post-signature. If Tessera grows headcount (they're already at 1,200 and moving upmarket), Clay could be forced to provision materially expanded usage at deep-discount pricing with no floor on volume. It also creates a list-price anchoring risk: if Clay adjusts 2027 pricing, Tessera's 2026 locked rate becomes a MFN-adjacent lever in renewal negotiations.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Expansion seats purchased during the Initial Term shall be priced at Clay's then-current list price for Customer's tier, less a discount of fifteen percent (15%). This expansion discount applies solely to net-new seats added beyond the committed seat count in this Order Form, is non-cumulative, and expires upon renewal or the end of the Initial Term, whichever occurs first. No pricing lock or most-favored-nation right is granted hereunder.
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: Accept a pricing lock capped at a maximum of [25]% above the contracted per-seat rate (not the full 35% discount), applicable only to expansions of up to 20% of contracted seat count, and expiring no later than December 31, 2026 (not 2027).
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Clay has granted limited in-term expansion discounts (10–15%) for strategic logos but has not locked 2026 discounted pricing through a subsequent calendar year at mid-market ACVs.

flagged_clauses.customer_proposed_language: Clay shall waive all implementation and onboarding services fees, estimated at $40,000, as a condition of executing this Order Form.
flagged_clauses.risk_explanation: Standalone, a $40K services waiver on a $240K ACV deal is a 16.7% additional concession on top of the 35% discount — effective realized rate is ~51% off list. More importantly, waiving implementation on a 3-vendor displacement with a new CRO and a 6-month free ramp increases churn risk: if onboarding fails without structured services, Tessera has both the TFC clause and a legitimate dissatisfaction argument to exit early.
flagged_clauses.suggested_counter: Implementation services (standard package, $40,000) shall be provided at no additional charge ('Complimentary Services') conditioned upon: (a) Customer completing all required data integrations and user provisioning within thirty (30) days of the Effective Date; and (b) Customer designating a named internal project owner with availability of no less than 10 hours per week during the Onboarding Period. Failure to meet these conditions releases Clay from the Complimentary Services obligation, and Clay may elect to provide services at standard rates.
flagged_clauses.fallback_position: If conditions cannot be imposed, accept the waiver but require a signed mutual onboarding success plan with milestone dates as an exhibit to the Order Form, making the 90-day TFC fallback (if accepted) contingent on Clay meeting those milestones.
flagged_clauses.precedent_notes: Implementation waivers have been approved for competitive displacement deals above $300K ACV with structured onboarding commitments; this deal is below that threshold.

standard_clauses_affirmed: Payment terms: annual upfront (affirmed per deal record)
Data residency: not raised, presumed standard US hosting
Indemnification: not flagged, presumed mutual standard
Warranty: not raised, presumed standard 90-day SLA
redline.one_line_summary: This deal cannot ship as-is: the combination of a 6-month free period, an anytime 90-day TFC, and a 2027 expansion pricing lock creates a scenario where Clay collects as little as $40K on a nominal $480K TCV contract — eliminate or hard-cap the TFC and compress the free period to 60 days before signing.
redline.reasoning_summary: The headline risk is the compounding of four aggressive clauses that individually are each precedent-stretching but together create a catastrophic downside: zero revenue for 6 months, an exit right exercisable as early as month 9 of the paid term, expansion volume locked at a 35%-discounted rate through 2027, and $40K in services absorbed with no onboarding commitment from the customer. Tessera's health score of 58, Series C capital efficiency pressure, and new CRO consolidation mandate all suggest real churn risk if the product doesn't land perfectly. The TFC clause must be eliminated or pushed past month 18 of the paid term as a hard deal condition; the free period should be compressed to 60 days. The expansion pricing lock and services waiver are negotiable with guardrails. Customer signals suggest genuine consolidation intent but also financial discipline pressure — Clay has real leverage here given it is displacing three vendors at once and should not price and structure as if it is the underdog.

required_approvers.rationale: Deal TCV of $480K does not exceed $1M, but this is a default rule that applies to all deals; however, the deal is blocked by Pricing guardrails requiring CFO sign-off on the 33.3% discount (exceeds 30% mid-market threshold) and 12-month ramp (exceeds 3-month mid-market ceiling).
Effective discount of 33.3% exceeds the 25% threshold, triggering mandatory CFO review.

required_approvers.rule_triggered: Non-standard payment terms route to Finance
required_approvers.rationale: Deal does not contain flagged deferred billing or net-60 terms in the explicit non_standard_clauses list, but the ASC 606 agent identified variable consideration and contract modification risk from the 90-day TFC clause and expansion pricing lock that require Finance scrutiny of cash recognition and deferral mechanics.
Deal ACV of $240K falls within the $100K–$500K band, mandating RevOps review for deal structure consistency.

Redline Agent marked overall_redline_priority as 'block'; the deal contains four high-risk non-standard clauses (6-month free period, 90-day TFC, expansion pricing lock, and implementation waiver) that Legal must resolve before signature.
AE Manager is the default first approver for all deals and must champion the deal internally before it advances to specialist reviewers.

blockers_to_address_first: Pricing guardrail block (absolute gross margin floor of 25% breached at 10% estimated margin): Deal structure must be reshaped before any approval can proceed. Pricing Agent provided three alternative structures (A: Remove Free Period + Re-scope PS; B: 36-month Commit at 25% Discount; C: Usage-Commit Hybrid). AE must select and re-quote one before resubmission.

Redline priority block: 90-day termination-for-convenience clause must be eliminated or pushed to month 18+ of paid term; 6-month free period must be compressed to maximum 60 days. Expansion pricing lock must be capped (e.g., 20% above contracted rate, max 20% of seat expansion, expiring Dec 31 2026). Implementation waiver must be conditional on customer onboarding commitments or paired with signed milestone plan.

ASC 606 contract modification risk: The 90-day TFC combined with expansion pricing lock creates enforceable-term and revenue-recognition uncertainty. CFO/auditor must pre-clear the final contract language before signature to validate revenue schedule and potential clawback exposure.

Mid-market pricing guardrail documentation: AE must provide documented competitive context (three-vendor bake-off, incumbent spend of ~$320K, 10-day decision window) to justify the 33.3% discount to CFO and Finance. Competitive loss scenario analysis should accompany resubmission.

Customer health and churn risk mitigation: Customer health score of 58 (mid-range) combined with aggressive cost-reduction posture and new CRO consolidation mandate raises churn risk if onboarding misfires. Redline fallback requires Clay to impose onboarding success milestones and condition TFC fallback (if accepted) on Clay meeting those milestones; this must be drafted as an exhibit before Legal can sign off.
approval.one_line_summary: This deal is structurally blocked on margin (10% vs. 25% floor) and redline risk (90-day out + 6-month free period + expansion lock) and cannot advance to approval chain until AE reshapes the pricing/concession stack and Legal hard-caps or eliminates the TFC clause, compresses the free period to 60 days, and ties implementation waiver to customer onboarding success metrics; if reshaped, it routes to AE Manager → RevOps + CFO + Finance + Legal (parallel, ~8 days).
approval.reasoning_summary: The deal cannot proceed in its current form due to an absolute margin breach (10% estimated vs. 25% floor) that makes it non-viable per the Pricing Agent's assessment; no approver can override this guardrail without a structural rewrite. The Redline Agent's 'block' priority on the TFC clause, combined with the ASC 606 agent's low-confidence estimate driven by contract modification risk, escalates Legal to a veto-capable step. RevOps and Finance are also required in parallel due to the aggressive discount and non-standard ramp. Once the AE reshapes the deal (using one of the Pricing Agent's three alternative structures), the approval chain becomes straightforward — AE Manager → parallel review by CFO, Finance, Legal, and RevOps — but the current submission is not approvable without substantial customer negotiation and internal deal restructuring.

text.text: Deal Review: Apollo/Outreach/ZoomInfo Displacement — Tessera Health

fields.text:
*Approval Chain*
AE Mgr → RevOps + CFO (veto) + Finance + Legal (veto) · ~8 days

text.text:
*What the deal team needs to know*

• *This deal is structurally blocked and cannot advance as submitted.* The ~10% estimated gross margin breaches the 25% absolute floor — no approver can override this. The concession stack (6-month free period, waived $40K implementation, 33.3% discount, 90-day anytime out, 2027 expansion pricing lock) compounds to a worst-case scenario where Clay collects as little as $40K on a $480K TCV contract if Tessera exits at month 9. The AE must select and re-quote one of the three alternative structures (A: Remove free period + re-scope PS at 20% disc; B: 36-month commit at 25% disc; C: Usage-commit hybrid) before resubmission.

• *The 90-day termination-for-convenience clause is a hard block requiring Legal veto clearance.* Combined with the 6-month free period it functionally converts a 24-month deal into a rolling quarterly commitment. Redline position: eliminate TFC entirely or push exercisability to month 18+ of the paid term; compress free period to 60 days max. The TFC also triggers ASC 606 contract-modification risk — the enforceable term may collapse to 90 days, materially altering revenue recognition and ARR reporting. CFO/auditor pre-clearance required before signature.

• *Competitive context is real but Clay has more leverage than the current structure implies.* Tessera's new CRO (hired Mar 2026) has a stated mandate to consolidate a $320K three-vendor stack, the VP RevOps RFP is focused precisely on Clay's category, and 64% of mid-market healthtech RevOps leaders cite consolidation as their #1 priority. Clay is displacing three incumbents simultaneously — it should not price and structure as the underdog. Documented competitive context (three-vendor bake-off, $320K incumbent spend, 10-day window) must accompany any resubmission to justify the discount to CFO and Finance.

elements.text: AE: Priya Shankar · AE Manager: Marcus Reilly · Close date: 2026-05-14 · review filed by Kiln · agent-driven

slack_post.plaintext_fallback:
DEAL REVIEW — Apollo/Outreach/ZoomInfo Displacement | Tessera Health
ACV: $240K | TCV: $480K | Discount: 33.3% | Est. Margin: ~10% (absolute floor: 25%) | Approval: AE Mgr → RevOps + CFO + Finance + Legal (~8 days)
STATUS: STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED. Deal cannot advance — absolute gross margin floor breached, redline priority block on 90-day TFC + 6-month free period. AE must select an alternative pricing structure and re-quote before resubmission.
Key risk: Concession stack (free period + waived $40K impl + 33.3% disc + 90-day out + 2027 pricing lock) creates a worst-case where Clay collects ~$40K on a $480K TCV contract. TFC clause may also collapse ASC 606 enforceable term to 90 days — CFO/auditor pre-clearance required.
AE: Priya Shankar | Mgr: Marcus Reilly | Close: 2026-05-14 | Filed by Kiln · agent-driven

ae_email_draft.subject: [Tessera Health] Deal desk review complete — BLOCKED, 3 actions required before resubmission
ae_email_draft.body_markdown:
Priya, the deal desk review on the Tessera Health displacement is complete, and this deal is **structurally blocked** — it cannot enter the approval chain in its current form. The concession stack (6-month free period + waived $40K implementation + 33% discount + 90-day anytime out) has collapsed estimated gross margin to ~10% against a 25% absolute floor, and Legal has flagged a block-level redline on the TFC clause. Here's what you need to do before resubmitting:

1. **Reshape the pricing structure.** Pick one of the three Pricing Agent alternatives (A: replace the free period with a 3-month paid ramp + $25K PS line; B: extend to 36 months at 25% discount; C: usage-commit hybrid with co-marketing credit in lieu of waived services) and re-quote. The current structure cannot be approved at any level — no escalation path overrides the margin floor breach.
2. **Kill or hard-cap the 90-day TFC clause.** Legal's hard counter: eliminate TFC during the Initial Term entirely, or push it to no earlier than month 18 of the paid term with a 50% remaining-fees termination penalty. This is a veto-capable Legal block and also triggers an ASC 606 enforceability issue that requires CFO/auditor pre-clearance before signature.
3. **Compress the free period to 60 days and document competitive context.** Bring the free period down from 6 months to 60 days (Clay's precedent ceiling for competitive displacement). Separately, prepare a written competitive justification (Apollo/Outreach/ZoomInfo bake-off, $320K incumbent spend, 10-day decision window) — CFO and Finance will require this to clear the 33.3% discount on resubmission.

Once reshaped, the approval chain is: **Marcus (AE Manager, Step 1) → RevOps + CFO + Finance + Legal in parallel (Step 2)**. CFO and Legal both hold veto. Budget 8 business days for the full cycle — given your May 14 close date, you need to resubmit a restructured deal **by end of day tomorrow** to have any chance of making that window.

customer_email_draft.subject: Tessera Health × Clay — revised structure for your review
customer_email_draft.body_markdown: We appreciate the consolidation mandate your team is driving and understand the pressure to make a decision within the next ten days — displacing three incumbent vendors simultaneously is a meaningful undertaking, and we want Clay to be the right long-term foundation for that move. That said, after a full internal review of the proposed terms, we need to be direct with you: the current structure — six months free, waived implementation, a 90-day termination right, and a 2026 pricing lock through 2027 — is not approvable as written, and we'd rather tell you that now than let it stall in our approval chain during your decision window. On the free period, we can offer a maximum 60-day onboarding period before the paid term commences; six months free at this ACV is outside what we've approved at any mid-market deal, and stacking it against an anytime exit right creates a scenario we can't accept. On the termination-for-convenience clause, we cannot offer a 90-day anytime-out during the initial term — our standard position is no TFC during the committed period, and if mutual trust requires a fallback, we'd consider a TFC right exercisable only after month 18 of the paid term, with a 50% remaining-fee buyout. On expansion pricing, we'll offer a 15% discount off then-current list for net-new seats added during the term, capped at 20% seat expansion above your committed count and expiring at renewal — the 2026-rate lock through end of 2027 is not something we can hold given the margin exposure if your headcount grows as projected. On implementation services, we'll retain the waiver as a goodwill gesture conditioned on Tessera designating a named internal project owner within the first 30 days — this protects both sides and gives your CRO a clean onboarding story. Our ask is straightforward: let's align on a reshaped term sheet by end of this week so we have runway to clear approvals before your May 14 deadline — can we get 30 minutes with you and your RevOps lead tomorrow to walk through the counterproposal?

sections.heading: Headline
sections.content_markdown: Clay is pursuing a new-logo competitive displacement of Tessera Health's three-vendor sales stack (Apollo + Outreach + ZoomInfo, ~$320K/yr incumbent spend) at $240K ACV / $480K TCV over 24 months. **The deal as submitted is structurally blocked and cannot be approved without a significant reshape of pricing and contract terms.**

sections.heading: Pricing Summary
sections.content_markdown:
| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| List Price | $360K ACV |
| Proposed Price | $240K ACV / $480K TCV |
| Headline Discount | 35% (effective 33.3%) |
| Estimated Gross Margin | ~10% |
| Absolute Margin Floor | 25% |

**Concession stack compounds the headline discount materially:**
- 6 months free (zero cash in months 1–6; worst-case cash collected = ~$40K if TFC exercised at month 9)
- $40K implementation services waived (16.7% additional effective discount)
- True effective discount vs. list, inclusive of free period + waived services: **~51% off list**

**Three restructuring alternatives exist (Pricing Agent):**
- **Alt A** — Remove free period; 3-month paid ramp; PS as $25K line item; 20% discount → 40% margin ✅
- **Alt B** — 36-month term; 25% discount; MFN-capped expansion lock; waived PS retained → 33% margin ⚠️
- **Alt C** — Usage-commit hybrid at $220K floor + overage; co-marketing credit replaces waived PS → 40% margin ✅

AE must select and re-quote one alternative before resubmission.

sections.heading: Risk Findings
sections.content_markdown:
**🔴 Hard Blocks (deal cannot move without resolution)**

1. **Gross margin absolute floor breached** — 10% estimated margin vs. 25% floor. Non-overridable; structural deal reshape required before any approval step.
2. **Discount exceeds 30% mid-market threshold** — 33.3% triggers mandatory Finance + CFO sign-off; cannot proceed without documented competitive context filed by AE.
3. **12-month ramp far exceeds 3-month ceiling** — Finance sign-off required; ramp structure obscures effective discount and creates ASC 606 complexity.
4. **90-day termination-for-convenience (TFC) clause** — Redline: HIGH risk. Converts $480K TCV into a rolling quarterly commitment. ASC 606: may collapse enforceable contract term to 90 days (ASC 606-10-25-3), collapsing the revenue schedule. CFO/auditor pre-clearance required. Legal veto applies.
5. **Redline overall priority: BLOCK** — Legal must resolve all four flagged clauses before signature.

**🟡 Warnings (require resolution or documented acceptance)**

6. **6-month free period** — Extends service delivery to 30 months across 3 fiscal years; ratable recognition of $480K TCV begins month 1. Counter: compress to 60-day onboarding period.
7. **Expansion pricing lock through Dec 2027** — May constitute a material right under ASC 606-10-55-41 requiring transaction price allocation. Creates MFN-adjacent leverage at renewal. Counter: cap at 15% discount, ≤20% seat expansion, expire Dec 2026.
8. **Implementation services waiver ($40K)** — Below $300K ACV precedent threshold for unconditional waivers; increases churn risk if onboarding fails without structured services. Must be conditioned on customer onboarding commitments.
9. **Customer health score: 58** — Mid-range; new CRO consolidation mandate + Series C capital efficiency pressure = elevated churn risk if product doesn't land. Onboarding success milestones must be a contract exhibit.
10. **ASC 606 confidence: LOW** — Interaction of TFC + expansion lock + free period + waived PS creates highly judgment-dependent recognition schedule. Auditor review required.

sections.heading: Approval Routing
sections.content_markdown:
**Step 1 (sequential):** AE Manager (Marcus Reilly) — must champion before escalation

**Step 2 (all parallel):**
| Approver | Trigger | Veto? |
|---|---|---|
| **CFO** | Discount >25%; ramp >3mo ceiling | ✅ Yes |
| **Legal** | Redline priority = BLOCK; TFC + expansion lock | ✅ Yes |
| **Finance** | ASC 606 variable consideration; non-standard ramp | No |
| **RevOps** | ACV $100K–$500K band | No |

**Estimated cycle time: ~8 business days** (assuming clean resubmission)

⚠️ **Clock has not started.** Deal cannot enter approval chain until AE resolves the margin block and Legal hard-caps or eliminates the TFC clause. Close date is May 14 — **9 calendar days from today**. Timeline is at serious risk.

sections.heading: Recommendation
sections.content_markdown:
**🔴 HOLD — Reshape Required Before Approval Can Begin**

This deal is non-approvable in its current form: the absolute gross margin floor is breached (10% vs. 25% minimum), the TFC clause is a Legal veto risk and an ASC 606 blocker, and the concession stack cumulatively represents ~51% off list with a catastrophic downside cash scenario (~$40K collected on a $480K TCV contract). AE must immediately select one of the three Pricing Agent alternative structures, compress the free period to ≤60 days, eliminate or push the TFC right past month 18 of the paid term, and tie the implementation waiver to signed onboarding milestones — only then can the deal enter the 8-day approval chain. Given the 10-day decision window, AE should initiate customer negotiation on the TFC and free period **today**.

comms.reasoning_summary: Calibrated customer email to firm tone given the deal context. AE email leads with verdict (ship-with-conditions (blockers must clear first)) and a numbered next-step list. One-pager and Slack post both headline the recommendation and surface 4 flagged redlines (4 countered in customer reply). Approval routing summary: ~8 business days through 5-step chain.

synthesis: Pricing blocks the deal as structured: a 33.3% effective discount and ~10% gross margin breach the 25% absolute floor, making it non-viable without a structural rewrite. ASC 606 flags four interacting complexities — the 6-month free period, waived implementation, 90-day TFC, and 2027 expansion lock — that push transaction price allocation and the recognition schedule into auditor-review territory. Redline rates the package "block," with the TFC clause as a hard condition that must be eliminated or pushed past month 18 and the free period compressed to 60 days. Approval cannot clear in current form (no override on the margin floor), so the call is hold and re-scope using one of Pricing's alternative structures before re-routing through the 8-day AE Manager → RevOps → CFO → Finance → Legal chain.

signals.headline: Tessera Health names new Chief Revenue Officer to lead consolidation push
signals.summary: Tessera Health announced the appointment of a new CRO (formerly VP Sales at a rival healthtech provider), tasked with consolidating sales tooling and accelerating mid-market enterprise expansion through 2026.

signals.headline: Tessera Health expands HIPAA-attested integration suite for hospital networks
signals.summary: Tessera released expanded HIPAA-attested integrations targeting Epic, Cerner, and Meditech-shaped buyers. The release is positioned as evidence of compliance maturity ahead of Tessera's enterprise upmarket push.

signals.headline: Tessera Health crosses 1,200 employees, posts second consecutive profitable quarter
signals.summary: Q4 2025 earnings update notes a 1,200-person headcount and a second straight profitable quarter, alongside a stated 2026 priority of vendor consolidation across go-to-market infrastructure.

signals.headline: Tessera Health adds VP RevOps from data-platform vendor
signals.summary: Tessera hired a VP RevOps from a data-platform vendor; her stated 90-day plan reportedly includes a sales-tooling RFP focused on prospecting, enrichment, and outbound orchestration.

signals.headline: Healthtech RevOps leaders cite stack consolidation as #1 2026 priority — survey
signals.summary: Industry survey finds 64% of mid-market healthtech RevOps leaders plan to consolidate three or more sales-engagement tools in 2026, citing renewal pressure and security-review fatigue.
